Article Title: Jammu and Kashmir: Kashmiri Pandit's eatery sparks 'ghar wapsi' calls
Article Text:
Akash Dhar (second from left), with his restaurant team

‘We want eatery to do well and hope this will encourage other Kashmiri migrants to return’

LANGATE (KUPWARA): At the centre of Langate's main market in north Kashmir's Kupwara district stands a brightly lit restaurant with modern decor and eight neatly arranged tables. In one corner, 29-year-old Akash Dhar welcomes customers with a smile. His eyes remain open though he cannot see.Two weeks ago, Dhar - whose Kashmiri Pandit family had migrated from J&K as violence peaked in the 1990s - tentatively opened 'Taste and Treats' restaurant.However, he was hardly ready for the response he would receive from the local community. "It has been incredible," he beamed."My landlord, Bashir Ahmad Beigh, won't take any rent from me and customers are trooping in," he told TOI.Starting a business back in Kupwara "felt like a natural step", said Dhar, who had made several attempts to qualify for the civil services but couldn't.After all, his mother Chandra Dhar is from Handwara, about 4km from Langate, and his father Ashish Dhar is a “Langate native”.The family left for Jammu three decades ago where Dhar was born in a migrant colony.Dhar’s vision began deteriorating when he was a child. His parents took him to Delhi, where doctors diagnosed him with retinitis pigmentosa, a degenerative genetic eye disease that typically begins with night blindness, eventually leading to total blindness.His younger sister has the same condition. Both, however, pursued higher education. Akash graduated from University of Jammu and completed professional computer courses in Delhi. His sibling is studying music. An accomplished cricketer, Dhar represented the J&K blind cricket team in 2022.“It seems like this (the restaurant) is what I had always been waiting for,” Dhar said, as one of his employees, 22-year-old Yasir Aamir, nodded approvingly. “A neighbour told me about this restaurant,” Aamir said. “I applied for the job and got it. I am happy, so is my family.”The restaurant’s manager, Irfan Ahmad Lone, is upbeat. “We are getting customers from different areas,” Lone said. “Yesterday we had guests from Ganderbal. There were Muslims and Kashmiri Pandits. Some came from Srinagar too.”Latief Ahmad Ganai (30), president of the local market association, thinks so too. “We all want Taste and Treats to do well,” Ganai said.“We hope this will encourage other Kashmiri migrants to return.”
Article Summary: Akash Dhar's Kashmiri Pandit family had migrated from J&K as violence peaked in the 1990s. His vision began deteriorating when he was a child. Doctors diagnosed him with retinitis pigmentosa, a degenerative genetic eye disease that typically begins with night blindness, eventually leading to total blindness.

Article Title: Indian tourist drowns while swimming in Nepal's Phewa Lake
Article Text:
Representative image (PTI file)

NEW DELHI: A 28-year-old Indian tourist from Bihar died after drowning in Nepal's Phewa Lake in Pokhara, police said on Friday.The deceased was identified as Rohit Kumar, who had travelled to Pokhara with four friends. The incident took place on Thursday evening after the group visited the Tal Barahi Temple, located on an island in the lake.According to the district police office, Kaski, the group had docked their boat at the temple when Rohit and one of his friends decided to swim in the lake. During the swim, Rohit reportedly sank into the water and went missing.A disaster management team led by armed police force assistant Sub Inspector Devendra KC, deployed from the Phewa Watch and Rescue Tower, launched a search operation immediately after receiving information about the incident.Rohit was rescued at around 5 pm by a team of the armed police force, Kaski district police spokesperson Birendra Kumar Paswan said.He was rushed to the Western Regional Hospital in Pokhara, where he succumbed during treatment, police said.Authorities are investigating the incident.
Article Summary: The deceased was identified as Rohit Kumar, who had travelled to Pokhara with four friends. The incident took place on Thursday evening after the group visited the Tal Barahi Temple, located on an island in the lake. Rohit was rescued at around 5 pm by a team of the armed police force.

Article Title: G'day Chennai! Australia's Big Bash opener to be held in Tamil Nadu this December
Article Text:
PM Modi gifts former Australia skipper Steve Waugh two-decade-old photograph during MCG visit

Cricket Australia hopeful of hosting IPL match in Oz

Australia's T20 competition, Big Bash League, will travel thousands of kilometres for its opening match between defending champions Perth Scorchers and Melbourne Renegades, which will take place in Chennai on Dec 12.Australian PM Anthony Albanese made the announcement after a meeting with PM Narendra Modi at Melbourne Cricket Ground. This will be the first instance of a foreign cricket league playing in India, said Albanese as he joined Modi in unveiling a sports collaboration map.Modi and Albanese were joined by former Australia captains Steve Waugh and Lisa Sthalekar for an event at MCG to celebrate India-Australia sporting ties.BBL's opening match at Chennai on Dec 12 will kick off a week-long G'day Namaste festival of Australian sport, culture and business engagement, said Prime Minister Anthony Albanese. He added that he was certain Australia would support India's bid for the 2036 Olympics.PM Modi welcomed the announcement, saying that for any sporting league, staging an event in India guarantees tremendous reach and viewership.Following the announcement, Cricinfo reported that Cricket Australia is hopeful this could pave the way for the hosting of an IPL match in Australia in the future."There's certainly been interest from time to time at state govt level and at venue level, and with some of our clubs to imagine what that could look like bringing an IPL game out here," said Alistair Dobson, executive general manager of Big Bash Leagues.Major T20 leagues have so far not been offshoring except, as the report said, when forced to, like in the IPL's case by the Covid pandemic. Cricket Australia was reported to be confident that the match would be the most-watched game in Australian domestic league history for any sport, eclipsing the rugby league state of origin decider that broke the local broadcast record with 6 million viewers.A Reuters report said that the announcement came amid opposition to private investment in the Big Bash, which critics say could lead to the tournament ceding control to IPL franchises, and that Cricket Australia hopes to open up the Big Bash League to private investment to boost its finances and improve competitiveness in T20 cricket.On the sports collaboration roadmap, Indian govt said it would promote cooperation in training and capacity building, sports science and technology, and sports industry and investment.
Article Summary: Big Bash League's opening match will take place in Chennai on Dec 12. Australian PM Anthony Albanese made the announcement after a meeting with PM Narendra Modi at Melbourne Cricket Ground. Albanese added that he was certain Australia would support India's bid for the 2036 Olympics.

Article Title: 30% deletions in Daman & Diu SIR draft roll
Article Text:
Representative image

NEW DELHI: The Union territory of Daman and Diu and Dadra and Nagar Haveli witnessed the deletion of 29.7% electors -- including a whopping 22.5% on account of being absent or untraceable -- in its draft roll published on Friday, a trend linked by SIR-related officials to the UT's large migrant population and highly industrialised status.Of the 4.28 lakh electorate of the UT on the day of announcement of SIR on June 4, 2026, barely a little over 3 lakh, or 70.3% turned in their enumeration forms. The remaining 29.7% were struck off the roll, 1.7% electors were found to have died, 4.6% permanently shifted, 0.6% registered at multiple places and 0.2% classified in the 'others' category. The most interesting figure, of course, was the 22.5% electors who had to be marked untraceable or absent as they were not found on their registered addresses despite multiple visits by the booth level officer (BLO)."The latest door-to-door survey by the health department had put the UT's population at 6.8 lakh, of which around 3 lakh were migrants, largely from states like Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, Rajasthan and Odisha. They come here for short-term work in the nearly 7,000 industries that make the UT a highly-industrialised zone. These migrants are usually on the move and do not stay here for long periods," an officer involved in SIR told TOI.In an official statement, the chief electoral officer attributed the 29.7% deletions to these electors having enrolled in other states/UTs, not being found in existence, not having submitted their forms till July 3 or being reluctant to register as an elector for some reason or the other.While 29.7% is the highest rate of deletion seen so far in any state/UT at the draft roll stage, it is not the final figure, as deleted names can be added back during the claims and objections period from July 10 to Aug 9. Also, fresh enrolments (through Form 6) will also lead to some additions to the roll.The final roll for Daman and Diu and Dadra and Nagar Haveli will be published on Aug 11.
Article Summary: The Union territory of Daman and Diu and Dadra and Nagar Haveli witnessed the deletion of 29.7% electors in its draft roll published on Friday. A trend linked by SIR-related officials to the UT's large migrant population and highly industrialised status. Of the 4.28 lakh electorate of the UT on the day of announcement of SIR on June 4, 2026, barely a little over 3 lakh turned in their enumeration forms.

Article Title: Clear space, clear mind
Article Text: Thirukkural with the Times explores real-world lessons from the classic Tamil text ‘Thirukkural’. Written by Tamil poet and philosopher Thiruvalluvar, the Kural consists of 1,330 short couplets of seven words each. This text is divided into three books with teachings on virtue, wealth, and love and is considered one of the great works ever on ethics and morality. The Kural has influenced scholars and leaders across social, political, and philosophical spheres. Motivational speaker, author and diversity champion Bharathi Bhaskar explores the masterpiece. Some time ago, I was in Pondicherry to participate in a meeting. One of the organisers invited me home. It was a modest old house with faint traces of French architecture. Yet, the moment I entered, I noticed that the house was extraordinarily neat. There was no shoe rack at the entrance, but the slippers stood perfectly aligned. The lady of the house invited me into the kitchen. Living rooms can be arranged for visitors; kitchens rarely lie, I believe. This one had utensils, vegetables, milagai vaththal and sundaikkai drying. Yet there was order everywhere, from the loft above to the flowers before the pictures of Sri Aurobindo and the Mother. Then she told me a story. Years ago, the Mother had once visited their home. The house was messy as most homes are. The Mother sat down but her eyes kept returning to a bureau and finally asked if she could open it. The moment it was opened, objects which had been pushed and crammed inside tumbled out. Along with the family, she began arranging them herself. That day, the family learnt something that was passed down to generations: order outside can create a certain quietness within. Cleanliness thereafter was not an act performed before guests arrived. It became a way of life. An organised space gives the mind room to breathe. Of course, friends disagree. “A house is not a museum,” they tell me. Fair enough. Some minds seem to produce their brightest ideas from beneath three newspapers, two coffee cups and one missing charger. But private clutter is one thing, and the way we leave a shared space is another. Some men turn street corners into urinals. Flight and train toilet floors are left wet. Bottles fly out of car windows. People leave food packets in buses and popcorn tubs in cinema theatres, because “someone will clean them”. And who is that “someone”? A person like us. Whose back aches. Who is waiting to go home. Who has children and worries. Who is really the ‘kuppaikaaran’, the one who litters or the one who cleans that mess? At the 2026 FIFA World Cup, after Japan’s defeat to Brazil, Japanese supporters stayed behind with garbage bags, collecting cups, wrappers and litter in the stadium. Japanese children grow up cleaning classrooms and shared spaces. There is a saying in Japan, tatsu tori ato wo nigosazu — 'a departing bird does not muddy the water’. Imagine our schools asking children to clean their classroom or playground. By the next morning, parents may besiege the management. Perhaps cleanliness is not really about dust, shoes or cupboards. It is about our relationship with spaces. Why are some people disturbed by disorder while others barely notice it? Why do some cultures clean even what others have left behind? Two thousand years ago, Valluvar found that the difference in people lie not in appearance, wealth or status, but in culture. In Kural 993, he says: “Uruppu Oththal Makkal Oppu Andraal; Veruththakka Panbu Oththal Oppadhaam Oppu.” “Among people, physiognomy is the same; But culture makes all the difference.” Perhaps the real measure of culture is not how beautifully we occupy a place, but how gently we leave it behind; for the unknown person who comes next.
Article Summary: Bharathi Bhaskar explores real-world lessons from the classic Tamil text 'Thirukkural' Written by Tamil poet and philosopher Thiruvalluvar, the Kural consists of 1,330 short couplets of seven words each.

Article Title: 'Cricket a shared passion': PM Modi visits Australia's Melbourne Cricket Ground
Article Text:
PM Modi, accompanied by Australian counterpart Anthony Albanese, visited MCG.

NEW DELHI: Prime Minister Narendra Modi, accompanied by his Australian counterpart Anthony Albanese, visited the Melbourne Cricket Ground, where he signed the jerseys of young cricketers and posed with Australia's official mascot, "Ruby the Roo. "Former Australian men's cricket team captain Steve Waugh also met PM Modi at the stadium."Stepping into the MCG evokes two simultaneous emotions for any Indian. First, the thrill of an India-Australia match and second, the realisation that, in both our nations, cricket is not merely a sport but a shared passion. Yet, today, there is no pressure of a 'last-over finish.' Instead, there is only the joy of the game, the warmth of our friendship, and the energy of future champions," PM Modi said.Earlier, PM Modi met Angus Taylor, Leader of the Opposition of Australia and held talks on growing strength of India-Australia ties.In a post on X, he said, "Met Mr. Angus Taylor, Leader of the Opposition of Australia. We had an engaging conversation on the growing strength of India-Australia ties and the many opportunities ahead. I deeply value the warmth and broad support that our partnership enjoys across Australia."India and Australia on Thursday elevated their strategic partnership by adopting the Joint Declaration on Defence and Security Cooperation (JDDSC) and the Maritime Security Collaboration Roadmap (MSCR), while also agreeing to operationalise their civil nuclear agreement to facilitate uranium exports to India.The two defence initiatives were among 18 major outcomes announced after Prime Minister Narendra Modi's meeting with his Australian counterpart Anthony Albanese during the Third India-Australia Annual Summit. The agreements span defence, maritime security, energy, cyber and critical technologies, education, mining, research and cultural cooperation, underscoring a significant expansion of the Comprehensive Strategic Partnership.Foreign Secretary Vikram Misri said the adoption of the JDDSC and the maritime roadmap reflected the need for the bilateral partnership to adapt to evolving geopolitical and strategic realities."These constitute recognition that the partnership must evolve to meet changing strategic circumstances, and they reflect a commitment to advancing our defence and security partnership," Misri said.
Article Summary: PM Narendra Modi visited the Melbourne Cricket Ground, where he signed the jerseys of young cricketers. PM Modi met Angus Taylor, Leader of the Opposition of Australia and held talks on growing strength of India-Australia ties. India and Australia elevated their strategic partnership by adopting the Joint Declaration on Defence and Security Cooperation.

Article Title: Did officials miss this? Why Bellingham's England goal vs Norway should NOT have stood after bizarre camera cable incident
Article Text:
Jude Bellingham celebrates England's victory over Norway in a World Cup quarterfinal soccer match in Miami Gardens, Fla., Saturday, July 11, 2026. (AP Photo/Julio Cortez)

England fight back after Norway's bright start

England's Jude Bellingham (10) scores their first goal past Norway goalkeeper Oerjan Nyland (1) during the World Cup quarterfinal soccer match between Norway and England in Miami Gardens, Fla., Saturday, July 11, 2026. (AP Photo/Marta Lavandier)

The camera cable controversy

What do the Laws of the Game say?

Connected ball technology also comes into focus

England march on despite the controversy

England's Harry Kane (9) and other England players celebrate at the end of the World Cup quarterfinal soccer match between Norway and England in Miami Gardens, Fla., Saturday, July 11, 2026. (AP Photo/George Walker IV)

England’s FIFA World Cup 2026 quarter-final victory over Norway has been overshadowed by a controversial moment involving Jude Bellingham’s first-half equaliser. Footage circulating after the dramatic 2-1 extra-time win has sparked debate over a possible incident in the build-up to the goal, with questions now being raised over whether play should have been stopped under the IFAB Laws of the Game.The quarter-final began with Norway carrying the momentum of their historic run to the last eight, and they were rewarded in the 36th minute.Andreas Schjelderup produced a superb finish from the left side of the penalty area to give Ståle Solbakken's side a deserved 1-0 lead, putting England under pressure heading into the closing stages of the first half.The Three Lions thought they had found their response in first-half stoppage time. Goalkeeper Orjan Nyland launched a long goal kick towards midfield, where Harry Kane won possession before the move developed through Anthony Gordon. Gordon then slipped an excellent through ball into the path of Jude Bellingham, who took two touches before calmly finishing beyond Nyland to level the score at 1-1 in the 45+2nd minute.At first glance, it appeared to be a brilliantly constructed equaliser. However, replays shown later raised a potentially significant question over the very beginning of the move.Footage broadcast by FOX Sports appeared to suggest that Nyland's goal kick may have clipped one of the overhead Spidercam cables suspended above the pitch.The apparent contact seemed to alter the ball's trajectory before it dropped into Harry Kane's path, allowing England to regain possession and launch the attack that eventually resulted in Bellingham's equaliser.Journalist Melissa Reddy later reposted the clip after the original FOX Sports video remained geolocked to viewers in the United States, bringing the incident to wider attention.Importantly, there remains no definitive evidence confirming that the ball actually struck the cable. Neither the match officials nor FIFA have officially stated that contact occurred.Nevertheless, the footage immediately became one of the biggest talking points of the match.According to the International Football Association Board (IFAB) Laws of the Game, if the ball strikes a ceiling or any fixture hanging over the field of play, including an overhead camera cable or wire, the referee must stop play.The correct restart in that situation is a dropped ball awarded to the team that last touched the ball at the point where contact with the fixture occurred.Former FIFA referee Mark Clattenburg, working as an analyst for FOX Sports during the World Cup, explained that if the ball had indeed struck the cable, the officials should have declared a dead ball and restarted play with a drop ball.However, such a decision would have depended on either the on-field officiating team or the VAR identifying the contact in real time. As no intervention occurred, play continued uninterrupted and Bellingham's goal was allowed to stand.Norway's frustration was evident after the incident. Manager Ståle Solbakken was seen engaged in a lengthy discussion with the match referee at half-time, while several Norway players appeared to point and gesture in protest as they reacted to the decision. However, neither Solbakken nor his players publicly confirmed the precise nature of their complaints.The incident has also put the spotlight on the connected ball technology used at the 2026 FIFA World Cup. Each official match ball contains a microchip that sends real-time data to the VAR system, tracking movement, speed and touches to assist decision-making. The technology has already influenced major calls during the tournament, including Sweden’s goal against Tunisia and Croatia’s disallowed late equaliser against Portugal. Whether it detected any contact with the overhead cable in Miami remains unknown.The debate over Bellingham's equaliser did not end the drama. Norway thought they had reclaimed the lead in the 56th minute when Torbjørn Heggem found the net, only for VAR to disallow the goal after determining that Erling Haaland had fouled Elliot Anderson during the build-up.The match eventually went into extra time, where Bellingham struck again in the 93rd minute. Morgan Rogers' long-range effort was parried into his path, allowing the Real Madrid midfielder to score his second goal of the night and seal a 2-1 victory.England's win sends Thomas Tuchel's side into the FIFA World Cup semi-finals, where they will face the winner of the remaining quarter-final between Argentina and Switzerland. Yet despite the result, much of the post-match discussion has centred on the controversial equaliser, with debate continuing over whether the apparent camera cable contact should have halted play before England's leveller.
Article Summary: England beat Norway 2-1 in extra-time in World Cup 2026 quarter-final. Jude Bellingham equalised after Andreas Schjelderup had given Norway a 1-0 lead. Footage has sparked debate over a possible incident in the build-up to the goal, with questions now being raised over whether play should have been stopped under the IFAB Laws of the Game.

Article Title: England vs Norway FIFA World Cup QF match result: Jude Bellingham's brace propels England into World Cup semifinals after extra-time win
Article Text:
Jude Bellingham scored a crucial extra-time goal for England's World Cup semi-final victory. Norway took an early lead through Andreas Schjelderup before a controversial moment aided England.(AP Photo)

A legendary performance from Jude Bellingham has fired England into the semi-finals of the FIFA World Cup 2026 after a breathless 2-1 extra-time triumph over Norway at the Hard Rock Stadium in Miami.On a night filled with high drama, bizarre technical controversies, and intense tactical battles, Bellingham proved to be the ultimate difference-maker. The Real Madrid superstar struck in the third minute of extra time, pouncing on a rebound after Orjan Nyland failed to clean up a venomous Morgan Rogers shot. The strike marked Bellingham’s sixth goal of the tournament, tying him with teammate Harry Kane and placing him just two behind frontrunners Kylian Mbappé and Lionel Messi.Norway, whose historic quarter-final run and charismatic "Viking row" celebrations had taken the internet by storm, struck first. In the 36th minute, Andreas Schjelderup stunned the possession-dominant Three Lions, firing a brilliant shot off the right post and into the back of the net.The match was turned on its head right before halftime through a moment of immense controversy. A Norwegian goal kick appeared to strike an aerial camera cable before dropping to Elliot Anderson, who quickly fed Bellingham for a clinical, low equalizer.By tournament rules, play should have been halted for a drop ball, but the infraction went unnoticed, sparking frantic protests from the Norwegian bench.Norway’s fortune dimmed further in the second half. In the 56th minute, Torbjørn Heggem bundled home a rebound to throw the stadium into a frenzy, only for VAR to cruelly disallow the goal due to an Erling Haaland foul in the box.England’s defense, anchored by Jordan Pickford, who pulled off a point-blank save against Haaland in the first half, successfully neutralized the Manchester City talisman, holding him goalless for the first time all tournament. By the second half of extra time, an exhausted Haaland was subbed off for Jorgen Strand Larsen, signaling the end of Norway's aerial threat.Sixty years of hurt have never felt closer to ending for England, who now march to Atlanta for Wednesday’s blockbuster semi-final against either Argentina or SwitzerlandEarlier, both teams observed a poignant moment of silence to honor 25-year-old South African midfielder Jayden Adams, whose tragic passing was announced on Saturday.
Article Summary: Jude Bellingham scored a crucial extra-time goal for England's World Cup semi- final victory. Andreas Schjelderup had given Norway the lead in the 36th minute. A controversial goal kick appeared to strike an aerial camera cable before dropping to Elliot Anderson.

Article Title: IND vs ENG 5th T20I: Shivam Dube's big blunder turns the game in England's favour - WATCH
Article Text:
Shivam Dube (Image credit: X)

England complete 4-0 series sweep



NEW DELHI: India made the perfect start in the fifth and final T20I against England by dismissing Phil Salt early at The Rose Bowl in Southampton. But just a few overs later, a costly fielding mistake from Shivam Dube gave England captain Harry Brook a lifeline.The incident happened in the sixth over of England's innings. Fast bowler Prince Yadav bowled a short delivery outside off stump, and Brook went for a big pull shot. Instead, he got a thick top edge that went high into the air. It looked like a straightforward catch for Dube, who ran back from the infield.However, he failed to judge the ball properly and dropped the big chance.The dropped chance proved to be a huge turning point.Brook was still in the early stages of his innings and made full use of the reprieve. He went on to smash an unbeaten 95 off just 46 balls, while Jos Buttler hammered 131 from 64 deliveries.The pair stitched together a stunning 233-run partnership for the second wicket, helping England post a massive 257.India's poor fielding did not end there. Dube later dismissed Buttler in the 19th over, but by then the England skipper had already done the damage.Brook was also dropped again on 90 by Ishan Kishan.Chasing a daunting target of 258, India started aggressively but could only manage 201/8 in 20 overs despite half-centuries from Ishan Kishan (56) and Tilak Varma (53).England sealed a 56-run victory to complete a 4-0 clean sweep in the five-match series.The defeat also cost India the No. 1 spot in the ICC Men's T20I rankings. England climbed to the top after extending India's losing streak in T20Is to six matches.
Article Summary: England complete 4-0 series sweep against India with 56-run win in Southampton. England captain Harry Brook hit 95 off 46 balls. Jos Buttler hit 131 from 64 deliveries. India could only manage 201/8 in 20 overs despite half-centuries.

Article Title: Who is Conor McGregor's wife, Dee Devlin? A closer look at her background, career, love life, and net worth
Article Text:
Who is Conor McGregor's wife, Dee Devlin? A closer look at her early life, career, love life, and net worth (Getty)

Meet Dee Devlin: The woman who stood by Conor McGregor from the start



What does Dee Devlin do?

What is Dee Devlin's net worth?

: These figures are estimates based on publicly reported financial information

How did Conor McGregor and Dee Devlin meet?

2026



The couple continue raising their four children while managing family and business interests together





For years, Dee Devlin has been one of the most recognisable faces beside UFC star Conor McGregor . Fans have seen her celebrate his biggest wins, support him through difficult moments and stand by him long before he became one of the biggest names in mixed martial arts. While McGregor enjoys the attention, Devlin has quietly built a reputation as his prime supporter and a key part of his personal and professional life.Their love life began years before championship belts, sold-out arenas and multi-million-dollar business deals. Dee Devlin has stood beside Conor McGregor through every stage of his rise in the UFC. The couple has built a family of four and grown together as their business interests expanded over the years. Here's a closer look at her early life, career, family, love story and estimated net worth.Dee Devlin was born on Aug. 9, 1987, in Walkinstown, a suburb in Dublin, Ireland. She is Irish and grew up with her two sisters, Sarah Devlin and Cathy Devlin. Unlike Conor McGregor, who has spent much of his life in the spotlight, Devlin has generally kept details about her parents and childhood private.She left school at the age of 15 and worked different jobs while trying to find her path. Before Conor McGregor became famous, Dee Devlin worked as a waitress, hairdresser and also took on various modelling assignments.Those early experiences shaped her work ethic and helped her become independent from a young age.Currently, she is widely known as Conor McGregor's wife, but she has also built her own public profile through business work, charity events and social media. Over the years, she has become one of the most recognised partners in the UFC world.As Conor McGregor's career grew, Dee Devlin became much more than a supportive partner. She gradually took on behind-the-scenes responsibilities and became involved in several aspects of his business operations.She has worked alongside Conor McGregor's management and promotional teams, helping with schedules, business planning, and media commitments. Devlin has also been involved with the promotion of Proper No. Twelve Irish Whiskey, one of McGregor's biggest business ventures.Away from business, she has often spoken about helping Conor McGregor prepare for fights. In previous interviews, she explained that she would cook meals, pack his gym bag, and make sure everything related to training ran smoothly so he could focus on training inside the gym.Despite her growing public profile, Devlin continues to keep much of her personal life away from the spotlight, choosing instead to focus on family and business.Dee Devlin's personal net worth is estimated to be between $15 million and $20 million as of 2026, based on publicly available financial estimates. Although she has not publicly confirmed her wealth, much of it comes from her involvement in Conor McGregor's business interests and long-term investments.Together, Conor McGregor and Dee Devlin are estimated to have a combined net worth of around $200 million to $220 million. Their wealth comes from several sources, including UFC earnings, endorsement deals, business investments, luxury properties and the successful sale of McGregor's majority stake in Proper No. Twelve Irish Whiskey.The power couple own luxury homes in Ireland and the United States and have invested in several business ventures beyond combat sports.Conor McGregor and Dee Devlin first met in 2008 at a nightclub in Dublin. At the time, McGregor was still chasing his dream of becoming a professional MMA fighter, while Devlin was working different jobs to support herself. The two quickly connected and began dating. Money was tight during the early years of their relationship, and there were times when the couple lived with McGregor's parents while he tried to establish himself in the sport.Dee Devlin supported him through those difficult years, both emotionally and financially. Conor McGregor has often spoken about how important she was during that period, saying she believed in him even when he had very little.As Conor McGregor's UFC career took off, Devlin remained by his side through title wins, setbacks, injuries and business success. The couple announced their engagement in August 2020 after more than a decade together, and later married in Dec. 2025.Now, they are parents to four children: Conor Jack Jr., Croía, Rían and Mack. Family remains an important part of their lives, and the children have regularly been seen supporting McGregor during major UFC events.As Conor McGregor gets ready for UFC 329, Dee Devlin's role in his story remains as important as ever. She has stood beside him through victories, setbacks, and life away from the Octagon helping build the family and business empire they share today. For fans, her journey is just as remarkable as McGregor's rise in the UFC.
Article Summary: Dee Devlin was born on Aug. 9, 1987, in Walkinstown, a suburb in Dublin, Ireland. She is widely known as Conor McGregor's wife, but she has also built her own public profile through business work, charity events and social media. Dee Devlin has stood beside Conor McGregor through every stage of his rise in the UFC. The couple has built a family of four and grown together as their business interests expanded over the years.

Article Title: How Jude Bellingham and girlfriend Ashlyn Castro kept their Raya romance completely private for months before the world noticed
Article Text:
Jude Bellingham and girlfriend Ashlyn Castro (Instagram)

Who is Ashlyn Castro and how did she meet Jude Bellingham ?

Has Ashlyn Castro confirmed her relationship with Jude Bellingham?

Has Ashlyn Castro been watching England at the 2026 World Cup?

Jude Bellingham has been one of England's most important players at the 2026 FIFA World Cup, helping the Three Lions into the semi-finals. But as his on-field performances continue to draw headlines, curiosity about his personal life has grown just as fast. The 22-year-old Real Madrid midfielder is dating American model and content creator Ashlyn Castro, 28, and the two have gone to considerable lengths to keep things quiet. Fans, however, have been paying close attention.Ashlyn Castro was born on December 17, 1997, in California, making her six years older than Bellingham. She has built a following of over 730,000 on Instagram as a model, influencer and content creator, working with brands like Clarins and appearing in DJ Khaled's music video for Popstar. Her grid leans into beauty, lifestyle and travel, with her bio offering nothing more than a green heart.The couple reportedly matched on Raya, the celebrity-exclusive dating app, sometime in late 2024. Neither has confirmed this publicly, but the timeline supports it. By November 2024, the two were already interacting on Instagram. Then in January 2025, they were photographed together having lunch in Madrid after Real Madrid's Champions League match against RB Salzburg.Shortly after, Ashlyn was spotted at the Bernabeu alongside Bellingham's mother, Denise, which said quite a lot without either of them saying anything at all.ID@undefinedCaption not available.ID@undefinedCaption not available.Not in any formal sense. Both have stayed conspicuously quiet about what is clearly a real, ongoing relationship. The confirmation has come through presence rather than posts. In February 2025, Ashlyn attended Real Madrid's match against Girona, sitting in the VIP section with Jude's family. That kind of access does not happen by accident.The most telling moment came around Jude's 22nd birthday. Ashlyn's Instagram Stories showed the two singing SZA's Snooze at what appeared to be a karaoke session, dancing together and sharing a kiss. It was the closest thing to a public declaration either has offered, and even then it was casual, almost incidental.The couple were also seen together at the Spanish tennis open in April, further confirming this is not a situationship the tabloids have been misreading.She has been present, and fans have noticed. Ashlyn was in the stands for England's 4-2 group stage victory over Croatia at Dallas Stadium on June 17. After the match, she and Jude were seen sharing a quick kiss in the stands, which many took as confirmation of everything that had been speculated about for months. She returned on July 1 for England's final group game against DR Congo, spotted arriving with other players' partners.She has not shared any of this on her Instagram, which fits the pattern entirely. The relationship exists in glimpses rather than announcements: a birthday video here, a pitch-side appearance there. For a footballer of Bellingham's profile, that kind of restraint is not accidental. It is a choice.Whether that changes as England's tournament run continues remains to be seen. The deeper they go, the harder it becomes to keep anything quiet.

Article Title: Who are Jude Bellingham’s parents? Inside Denise and Mark Bellingham’s role in shaping the England star
Article Text:
Jude Bellingham’s parents (Image Via Getty)

Who are Jude Bellingham’s parents Denise and Mark Bellingham?



What do Denise and Mark Bellingham do and are they still married?

Jude Bellingham has become one of England’s biggest football stars, but he has often said his success would not have been possible without his parents, Denise and Mark Bellingham. From driving him to training sessions to making big career decisions, they have played a huge role in every step of his journey. As England continues its FIFA World Cup 2026 campaign, many fans want to know more about the couple who helped raise one of football’s brightest talents.Denise and Mark Bellingham have supported Jude since he first started playing football as a child. They also have another son, Jobe Bellingham, who followed the same path and now plays for Borussia Dortmund after spells with Birmingham City and Sunderland.The family raised both boys in Stourbridge near Birmingham. As Jude’s career grew, they made major sacrifices to help both sons chase their dreams. When Jude joined Borussia Dortmund in Germany in 2020, Denise moved with him so he could settle into a new country.At the same time, Mark stayed in England with Jobe, who was still playing for Birmingham City. Later, when Jobe signed for Sunderland, Mark moved with him to continue supporting his younger son.Jude has never hidden how much his parents mean to him. Speaking to The Guardian in 2020, he said: “Before being great parents, they’re great people. I’ve grown up around them and seen the way they interact with other people, seen the way they treat people. When you have people like that, you don’t have to be told how to behave, you pick that up from them.”When he was presented as a Real Madrid player in 2023, Jude also praised his mother, saying: “Without my mum, sometimes I’d get too low with the lows or too high with the highs, and I stay pretty humble because I’ve got her around. It’s also great to have her there because she’s a great laugh as well. We get on so well and we’re always doing stuff together.”Before becoming involved in his sons’ football careers, Mark Bellingham worked as a sergeant with West Midlands Police. He was also a well-known non-league footballer and played for several clubs well into his forties.Jude has described him as one of his first football heroes because watching his father play helped him fall in love with the game.After Jude and Jobe’s careers took off, Mark left the police force and began helping manage their careers. Denise comes from a Human Resources background and is widely seen as the person who keeps the family’s day-to-day planning, finances, and travel organized.Together, they also backed Jude’s biggest career moves, including his transfers to Borussia Dortmund and later Real Madrid instead of staying in the Premier League.Former coach Kevin Betsy praised Mark’s influence while speaking to The Evening Standard, saying: ”[Jude’s dad] comes from the police background, so he’s very clear and concise, very disciplined, and that reflects on Jude and Jobe.”Reports from German newspaper Bild in 2025 claimed Denise and Mark had separated after more than 20 years of marriage after they were seen sitting apart during a Borussia Dortmund match.However, neither of them has publicly confirmed the reports. Despite the speculation, both continue to support Jude and Jobe and remain closely involved in their football journeys.
Article Summary: Denise and Mark Bellingham have supported Jude since he first started playing football as a child. They also have another son, Jobe Bellingham, who followed the same path and now plays for Borussia Dortmund. Denise comes from a Human Resources background and is widely seen as the person who keeps the family’s day-to-day planning, finances, and travel organized.

Article Title: Quote of the day by Melinda French Gates: “Philanthropy to me means using your voice, your time, your skills, or your money, your resources, to change the world for the better” - a timeless lesson tha
Article Text:
Bill Gates and Melinda French Gates

Quote of the day by Melinda French Gates

“Philanthropy to me means using your voice, your time, your skills, or your money, your resources, to change the world for the better”

Understand the meaning behind the quote by Melinda Gates

How Melinda French Gates reshaped the conversation around giving

Why generosity begins long before writing a cheque

The quiet acts that often leave the deepest impact

How anyone can become a philanthropist

Other memorable quotes by Melinda French Gates

"A woman with a voice is, by definition, a strong woman. But the search to find that voice can be remarkably difficult."

"When you invest in women and girls, you're investing in the people who invest in everyone else."

"It's the mark of a backward society, or a society moving backward, when decisions are made for women by men."

"Poverty is not being able to protect your family."

Why this message matters more than ever

Most people picture a philanthropist as someone signing enormous cheques, not someone volunteering an evening a week or simply speaking up for a cause they believe in. Melinda French Gates has spent years pushing back against that narrow picture. "Philanthropy to me means using your voice, your time, your skills, or your money, your resources, to change the world for the better," she has said, listing money almost as an afterthought rather than the whole point. For someone who co-chairs one of the largest charitable foundations in the world, that ordering says something worth paying attention to. It suggests that the biggest barrier to doing some good was never really a lack of money in the first place, but a much simpler, more common excuse: waiting until you feel like you have enough to give.The quote widens what philanthropy actually means. Most people hear the word and think immediately of large donations. French Gates lists voice, time, skills and money side by side instead, refusing to treat money as the only contribution that counts.Voice covers speaking up for fairness, education or healthcare, the kind of ordinary courage that has driven reform throughout history long before any money changed hands.Time is the resource almost everyone actually has, whether that means mentoring, volunteering or simply showing up for someone. Skills matter just as much.A teacher, a nurse, an engineer, all of them have expertise that helps people in ways a donation alone cannot.Money comes last in her list, and that ordering is not an accident. It reinforces the idea that generosity starts with willingness, not wealth. How much someone gives matters far less than whether they were willing to give something at all.Public conversations about philanthropy have traditionally centred on fundraising totals and headline donations. Through decades of work on global health, education and women's empowerment, French Gates met plenty of people changing lives without much money behind them at all, community health workers reaching isolated villages, teachers keeping girls in school, volunteers running local literacy programmes.Those experiences shaped how she talks about giving now. She has described looking at your own time, your own expertise, and your own voice as three separate categories worth activating on their own, not simply waiting until you have spare money to give away. That framing turns an intimidating idea into a much simpler question: what can you actually contribute today with what you already have.A common assumption is that meaningful giving only starts once someone becomes financially comfortable. In practice, communities have always been held together by people who contributed long before they had much spare money at all.Think of a teacher staying an extra hour to help a student who has fallen behind, or a retired professional passing on decades of experience to someone just starting out. None of that depends on a large donation, and each one can genuinely change the direction of someone else's life. The same applies across almost every profession, a nurse who takes the time to properly reassure an anxious patient, a lawyer who gives free advice to someone who could never otherwise afford it. None of it shows up in a donation report, and much of it lasts for years.History tends to remember dramatic gestures, record-breaking donations, and landmark discoveries. Most real change comes from something far quieter. A child who gets genuine encouragement from a teacher discovers confidence they did not know they had. A young professional given proper guidance by a more experienced colleague finds the courage to take a real career risk.None of that makes headlines, but it is exactly the kind of contribution French Gates is pointing at. Communities where people actually know each other and show up for one another tend to hold together better under real pressure, whether that pressure comes from economic hardship or a public health crisis. Strong relationships end up mattering just as much as money in moments like that.The word philanthropist usually conjures someone whose name sits on a hospital wing. French Gates offers a far more ordinary version of it. A parent organising a neighbourhood clean-up is practising philanthropy. A student volunteering to tutor children at the weekend is practising philanthropy. A small business owner offering an apprenticeship to a young person just starting out is doing the same thing, whether or not anyone ever calls it that.The point is accessibility. Nobody needs to wait until they are wealthy to start contributing something real. Some people offer creativity, others patience, others years of hard-won practical knowledge. None of that shows up neatly on a balance sheet, but it changes lives just the same.Faced with problems as large as climate change or global poverty, it is easy to feel like one person's contribution barely counts. French Gates' quote pushes back on that feeling directly. Lasting progress is rarely the result of one heroic act. It is usually the sum of a lot of ordinary ones, someone donating what they can, someone else sharing what they know, someone else simply showing up consistently over time.That is really the lasting point behind her words. Changing something for the better rarely requires vast wealth or global influence to begin with. Often it starts with a skill offered freely, an hour given without being asked, or simply the willingness to speak up when it would be easier to stay quiet.
Article Summary: Philanthropy to me means using your voice, your time, your skills, or your money, your resources, to change the world for the better. Most people picture a philanthropist as someone signing enormous cheques, not someone volunteering an evening a week. Melinda French Gates has spent years pushing back against that narrow picture.

Article Title: Mauritius spent more than 40 years removing invasive rats, cats and goats from its offshore islands, helping endangered seabirds return after decades
Article Text:
Why invasive species pushed Mauritius seabirds to the edge

The long fight to save Round Island

Clearing rats and cats from Ile aux Aigrettes

Clearing the northern islets one by one

Seabirds slowly returning to their old nesting grounds

A conservation model still being used today

Off the coast of Mauritius sit a string of small, mostly uninhabited islands that once told a very different story than they do today. For decades, animals brought there by ships, rats, cats, goats and rabbits among them, quietly wiped out native plants and drove seabird colonies to near collapse. Then, starting in the late 1970s, conservationists and the Mauritian government began a long, painstaking effort to remove these invasive species from islands like Round Island, Ile aux Aigrettes and Gunner's Quoin. It took decades of careful work, but the results speak for themselves. Seabirds that had all but vanished from these shores are now nesting again, and native forests are slowly reclaiming ground they had lost more than a century ago.Mauritius has no native land mammals apart from bats, so when sailors and settlers introduced rats, goats, rabbits and cats over the centuries, the local wildlife simply had no defences against them. Rats in particular proved devastating for seabirds, since they raid nests and eat eggs and chicks long before the young birds ever get a chance to fledge. According to a government report from the National Parks and Conservation Service of Mauritius , invasive alien species remain the single biggest threat to the country's native biodiversity, and this combination of introduced predators pushed several seabird populations to the brink across the small offshore islands.Round Island, a small volcanic islet roughly 22 kilometres north of the mainland, became one of the earliest and most important restoration efforts in the country. For more than 150 years, introduced goats and rabbits stripped the island of its vegetation, causing severe soil erosion and wiping out large parts of its native plant life. Working with the Durrell Wildlife Conservation Trust, conservationists managed to remove the goats by 1979 and finally cleared out the rabbits by 1986, according to the Mauritian Wildlife Foundation . Once these grazing animals were gone, the island's palm forests slowly began to recover, and with them came a genuine revival of native reptiles and seabirds that depend on that habitat to breed.A similar transformation took place on Ile aux Aigrettes, a small coral island just off the southeast coast of Mauritius. Since the 1980s, teams worked steadily to remove rats, cats and invasive plants from the island, finally eliminating these key predators by the early 2000s. With that threat gone, seabirds began returning to nest successfully, including species like the white tailed tropicbird and the wedge tailed shearwater, both of which had struggled for years under constant predation. The island's vegetation also responded well once rats were no longer around to damage seeds and young plants, allowing native forest cover to slowly rebuild itself.Beyond Round Island and Ile aux Aigrettes, Mauritius also tackled a cluster of smaller northern islets through a coordinated, multi year programme. Norway rats and hares were removed from Gunner's Quoin, ship rats were cleared from Gabriel Island, and mice were eliminated from Ile aux Cocos and Ile aux Sables, with cats and additional rodents later removed from Flat Island as well. These eradications, carried out by wildlife management specialists working with the Mauritian government, were slow and methodical, often involving specially designed bait stations placed across entire islands to make sure no invasive predators survived the process.The payoff from all this work has been steady rather than instant, but seabird numbers on these islets have genuinely climbed as the threat from predators disappeared. Species like wedge tailed shearwaters and red and white tailed tropicbirds have re-established nesting colonies on islands where they had once been pushed out entirely, taking advantage of restored vegetation and predator free ground to breed safely again. Reptile populations on these islands have benefited too, with several rare, endemic species now able to survive and even be reintroduced to islands where they had previously disappeared.What makes this story stand out is not just the outcome but the approach behind it. Restoring these islands required sustained cooperation between the Mauritian government, the Mauritian Wildlife Foundation, the Durrell Wildlife Conservation Trust and the country's Forestry Service, spread out across several decades rather than a single quick fix. This same island restoration model continues today across roughly a dozen offshore islands, and it has become something of a reference point for conservationists working on similar rewilding projects elsewhere in the world. While invasive species like monkeys still pose an ongoing challenge on the Mauritian mainland, the country's offshore islands now stand as a genuine example of what patient, well coordinated conservation work can achieve for species that were once staring down extinction.
Article Summary: Mauritius has no native land mammals apart from bats, so when sailors and settlers introduced rats, goats, rabbits and cats over the centuries, the local wildlife had no defences against them. Starting in the late 1970s, conservationists and the Mauritian government began a long, painstaking effort to remove these invasive species. Seabirds that had all but vanished from these shores are now nesting again, and native forests are slowly reclaiming ground they had lost more than a century ago.

Article Title: What will Dianna Russini do when The Athletic report finally drops? One NFL podcaster claims he already knows
Article Text:
Dianna Russini's future (Getty Images)

Why is Dianna Russini staying silent after The Athletic investigation began? **

Dianna Russini's exit from The Athletic was just the beginning. Months after photos of the veteran NFL insider and New England Patriots head coach Mike Vrabel emerged from an Arizona resort, the fallout continues to unfold in real time. Russini has largely gone quiet, but the silence may not hold much longer. The Athletic is now conducting a thorough review of her journalism career, and whatever that investigation surfaces is going to force her hand, one way or another.Dianna Russini's next move has become a subject of constant debate, but many observers believe she has little to gain by speaking before the review reaches its conclusion. With attention fixed on every development, any public statement could become part of the ongoing discussion before all the facts are known.The controversy intensified after photos allegedly showing Russini and Vrabel together at a resort in Arizona surfaced online. Since then, the fallout has unfolded very differently for the two figures. Vrabel has continued facing questions from reporters and has remained visible around the NFL, while Russini has largely avoided public appearances.That silence has only fueled speculation. Reports suggest The Athletic is conducting a detailed examination of Russini's work and professional conduct, making the outcome of the investigation one of the biggest unanswered questions surrounding her future.The conversation has become even more complicated after online users challenged portions of an early report published by The New York Times. Social media users pointed to what they described as inconsistencies, adding another layer of debate to an already closely watched story. Whether those claims ultimately affect the broader investigation remains unclear, but they have increased public interest in whatever findings emerge next.NFL podcaster Tony Farmer believes patience is Russini's strongest option. Rather than trying to defend herself while the investigation remains active, he argues that waiting could give her a better opportunity to respond with full context.Making a post on X, the podcaster explained, “I have no idea of Dianna Russini’s intentions, but if I had to guess, I’d say she will not be making any public comment or appearance until after The Athletic’s investigation into her journalism.”Farmer also shared why he believes that approach makes strategic sense.“IMO (in my opinion), it makes sense for her to play defense here. See what they know/say about her publicly and then poke holes in the claims later.”Whether Dianna Russini follows that path remains unknown. If she eventually decides to speak, her response will almost certainly face intense public scrutiny. Until then, the investigation is likely to shape every conversation about her future in sports media, with both supporters and critics waiting to see what evidence emerges before drawing lasting conclusions.
Article Summary: Dianna Russini has largely gone quiet after photos of her with Mike Vrabel surfaced. The Athletic is conducting a thorough review of her journalism career. Many observers believe she has little to gain by speaking before the review reaches its conclusion.
